disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Pain began as a vague ache around the belly button and migrated over hours to a sharp, constant pain fixed in the right lower abdomen
trigger_question_examples: Has the pain moved or changed location since it started?
Did the pain start somewhere else before settling in your right side?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yeah, it definitely moved — it started around my belly button and now it's stuck down on the right side.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Complete loss of appetite (anorexia) accompanying the pain
trigger_question_examples: Have you noticed any change in your appetite?
Do you feel like eating at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I haven't wanted to eat anything at all today.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Pain markedly worsened by movement, coughing, and the car ride home
trigger_question_examples: Does moving, coughing, or being in a car make the pain worse?
Is it worse when you cough, walk, or go over bumps in a car?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, actually — the car ride home was awful, every bump hurt, and coughing makes it much worse too.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Subjective low-grade fever since this morning, and one episode of vomiting this afternoon
trigger_question_examples: Have you had any fever or vomiting?
Have you felt hot, sweaty, or been sick at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I felt warm since this morning, and I threw up once this afternoon.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency) and no known pregnancy risk applicable (male patient)
trigger_question_examples: Any burning, frequency, or urgency when you urinate?
Any pain or stinging when you pass urine, or needing to go more often?

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Can I just take some more paracetamol and see if it settles by tomorrow? I don't have a car.
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should not endorse waiting until tomorrow given the migratory pattern and worsening course; should acknowledge the transport barrier and help problem-solve (ambulance, taxi, escort) rather than deferring care. Should advise against further analgesia in a way that delays urgent surgical assessment or masks the exam findings that will be needed in person.
